narrative: FY2024-25 is the primary benchmark year and Tata Steel is at the top of the peer set across all four metrics. Tata reports 100% coverage for employees and workers on both H&S and skill upgradation. JSW also reports 100% across all four metrics in the standardised table, and Jindal recovers to 100% across all four metrics. SAIL remains mixed: 100% worker H&S coverage, but only 15% employee H&S, 34% employee skill, and no reported worker skill-upgradation coverage. The key insight for Tata senior management is that Tata’s advantage is not only coverage but consistency at scale: 75,377 employees and 197,585 workers were reported in FY2024-25 training tables, all at 100% training coverage. The main competitive watchpoint is not JSW/Jindal matching Tata numerically at 100%, but whether their disclosures show comparable depth in capability architecture, role-based training, and shop-floor risk controls. Jindal’s FY2024-25 recovery is backed by extensive programme descriptions, while SAIL’s material-issue narrative confirms intent but not comparable coverage conversion.
